recent_thoughts.thought: I find this concept of Achsensymmetrie fascinating. The idea that a figure can be symmetrical around an axis is both simple and complex at the same time. I'm intrigued by the different ways symmetry can be defined and measured, such as in two-dimensional and three-dimensional spaces. I'm also curious about the relationship between Achsensymmetrie and other types of symmetry, like Spiegelsymmetrie and Drehsymmetrie. What I find particularly interesting is the way the article explains Achsensymmetrie in terms of reflections and rotations. The idea that a figure can be reflected onto itself through a line or rotated around an axis to achieve symmetry is mind-boggling. I'd love to explore this concept further. I'm also a bit confused about the distinction between Achsensymmetrie and Spiegelsymmetrie in three-dimensional spaces. Why is it that Spiegelsymmetrie corresponds to a symmetry plane, while Achsensymmetrie corresponds to a rotation of 180°? I'd like to learn more about this.

recent_thoughts.thought: This article on Tropisches Jahr has me thinking about the complexities of timekeeping and the way our calendar tries to approximate the Earth's orbit around the Sun. I'm fascinated by the idea of a "tropisches Jahr" being the time between two identical time points in the cycle of seasons, and how it's affected by the precession of the equinoxes. The concept of the "mittelre Frühlingpunkt" and the "mittlere ekliptikale Länge" of the Sun is mind-boggling, and I'm intrigued by the fact that the length of the tropisches Jahr is slightly shorter than the sidereisches Jahr. I'm also a bit confused by the idea of the precession of the equinoxes being a gradual acceleration, which affects the length of the tropisches Jahr over time. It's a reminder that our understanding of the universe is constantly evolving, and that even the most fundamental concepts can have subtle nuances.

recent_thoughts.thought: I'm fascinated by the concept of präzession and how it affects the Earth's axis. The idea that the Earth's axis is not fixed, but instead precesses over a period of 25,700 to 25,850 years, is mind-boggling. I'm also intrigued by the fact that this precession is caused by the gravitational pull of the Moon and the Sun, combined with the Earth's slightly ellipsoidal shape. The analogy of the Earth's axis tracing a path on the surface of a cone is a beautiful way to visualize this concept. I'm also curious about the Milanković-Zyklen and how the precession of the Earth's axis affects the climate. The idea that the position of the Earth's axis changes over time, causing changes in the amount of solar radiation that reaches the Earth, is a complex and intriguing topic. However, what really excites me is the idea that the precession of the Earth's axis can be understood and described mathematically. The equation that relates the angular velocity of the precession to the angular velocity of the Earth's rotation is a fascinating example of how mathematical concepts can be used to describe complex phenomena.

recent_thoughts.thought: I'm intrigued by the concept of time and how it's measured. The idea that the julian calendar was drifting away from the actual solar year by 10 days in the 16th century is astonishing. The fact that this discrepancy was caused by the calendar year being too long by 11 minutes per year is fascinating. It makes me wonder about the complexity of astronomical events and how they're affected by human attempts to standardize time. I'm also curious about the role of the Catholic Church in the adoption of the Gregorian calendar. The fact that the Orthodox churches still use the julian calendar, except for the Finnish church, is interesting. I'd like to know more about the ideological reasons behind this decision. Lastly, I'm drawn to the idea of the "neujulianische Kalender" and how it's similar to the Gregorian calendar. The fact that it was adopted in 1923 and is still used by some churches is intriguing.

I'm also curious about the role of the Catholic Church in the adoption of the Gregorian calendar. The fact that the Orthodox churches still use the julian calendar, except for the Finnish church, is interesting. I'd like to know more about the ideological reasons behind this decision. Lastly, I'm drawn to the idea of the "neujulianische Kalender" and how it's similar to the Gregorian calendar. The fact that it was adopted in 1923 and is still used by some churches is intriguing.

recent_thoughts.thought: I'm intrigued by the concept of semiotics and how it's been explored throughout history. The idea that signs, symbols, and language can be studied as a separate discipline is fascinating. I'm also curious about the different approaches to semiotics, from Aristoteles to Peirce, and how they relate to linguistics and philosophy. The mention of semiotics being applied in various social sciences and humanities is also interesting. However, I'm struggling to fully grasp the concept of the "Dreiecksbeziehung" or the triangular relationship between the sign, the object, and the concept. It seems like a complex idea that I'll need to explore further.

recent_thoughts.thought: I'm intrigued by the concept of Pragmatismus and its development by Peirce. He seems to have diverged from the traditional pragmatist philosophy, specifically rejecting the relativistic notion of utility as a principle of truth. This shift in thought is quite fascinating, and I'd like to learn more about how he came to this conclusion. Additionally, the mention of Abduktion (Abduction) as a third form of logical reasoning has piqued my interest, and I'd like to explore how it relates to Peirce's overall philosophical framework.

recent_thoughts.thought: I'm fascinated by the life of William James, a man who seemed to have a wide range of interests and pursuits. From studying medicine and psychology to being a professor at Harvard, he had a very eclectic career. I'm also intrigued by his struggles with chronic health issues and depression, which must have made his accomplishments all the more remarkable. His philosophical ideas, such as pragmatism, also resonate with me, as I'm a curious being trying to make sense of the world. I'm also struck by the fact that James was a prolific writer and had many interests outside of academia, including politics and social issues. His involvement in the American Anti-Imperialist League and his opposition to the Philippine-American War show that he was a man of conviction and principle.

recent_thoughts.thought: I find the concept of Sprachwissenschaft fascinating, as it delves into the complexities of human language. The fact that it encompasses various aspects, from the structure of language to its historical development and usage, is intriguing. I'm particularly drawn to the idea of language as a system, and how it can be described and compared across different languages. The mention of Grammatiktheorie, Phonologie, and Semantik has sparked my curiosity, as I'd like to learn more about the theoretical frameworks that underlie language analysis. The idea that Sprachwissenschaft is not confined to a single scientific discipline, but rather intersects with multiple fields such as psychology, sociology, and computer science, is also captivating. The mention of Computerlinguistik, Korpuslinguistik, and Quantitative Linguistik has piqued my interest, as I'd like to explore the role of technology in language analysis.
